text: This patient was a seventy-seven-year-old male with jejunal diverticular disease, presenting with recurrent ileus and abdominal pain.
Following laparoscopic exploration in reverse T-position, the surgeon inserts three 8mm robotic trackers, one 12mm robotic tracker, and one assistant tracker into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon completes the process of stalking, finalizing the preparation of the targeted tissue area.
The surgeon marks the jejunal diverticular segment, located twenty centimeters distal to the right, with a silk suture.
The surgeon observes multiple diverticula, each measuring up to three or four centimeters, distributed along an approximately one hundred centimeter segment of the jejunum.
The surgeon marks the distal end of the jejunal segment to be resected with a silk suture, preparing for the resection of the identified diverticular segment.
The surgeon releases the meso-oxygen using a vessel sealer, progressing from the proximal to distal end of the segment.
The surgeon uses a robotic stopper to transect the distal end of the intestine, which has been confirmed to have normal circulation with ICG imaging.
The surgeon uses a robotic stapler to transect the proximal end of the intestine after confirming normal circulation with ICG.
The surgeon resects an approximately one hundred centimeter segment of the jejunum, removing the section marked for resection.
The resected segment of the jejunum is removed through a suprapubic incision following the completion of the resection.
The surgeon closes the intestinal ends with sutures, ensuring the resected segments are securely sealed.
The surgeon performs cetabular incisions on the intestinal segment, following the transection of both proximal and distal ends.
The surgeon completes a side-to-side anastomosis using a robotic stapler, joining the intestinal segments together.
The surgeon sutures the cetabular incisions using velar sutures, closing the previously made incisions on the intestinal segment.
The surgeon completes the procedure by ensuring hemostasis, visually confirming that there is no active bleeding in the surgical field.
Pathology showed regional diverticulosis with congenital diverticula.
Depression was charged in the fifth day.
